issue.title: `BytecodeClasses.toString()` and `root()` throw `NullPointerException` when `input` is null while `classes()` provides a descriptive error
issue.body:
What happens: `BytecodeClasses.classes()` (line 84) guards against a null `input` field with `Objects.isNull(this.input)` and throws an `IllegalStateException` with a clear message. However, `toString()` (line 43) calls `this.input.toString()` and `root()` (line 53) returns `this.input` directly, both without any null check. Any caller that invokes `root()` or `toString()` on a `BytecodeClasses` instance constructed with a null path (e.g. when Maven does not configure the sources directory) will receive a bare `NullPointerException` with no diagnostic information, while the same situation inside `classes()` produces a helpful error message.

What should happen: `toString()` and `root()` should either apply the same null guard as `classes()` or, consistent with the "no null" principle, the constructor should reject null with `Objects.requireNonNull` so the error is caught at construction time with a clear message.

comments.body: @yegor256, please take a look when you have a moment.
